comments.text: I have found a construction disproving the stronger conjecture in literature here from 2002: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0012365X01001340 The construction provided here is identical to the one used by DeepMind, so I wouldn't consider this an original solve by the agent. I was suspect of the originality of the quite simple construction DeepMind had provided, and I also had a gut feeling I had seen this exact construction before, but I couldn't place it. I was able to find this literature source from Google AI results from searching for "8 vertex configuration local 3 distance set erdos". (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
On behalf of the DeepMind team, thank you for pointing this out!

Thanks for the acknowledgement, SwaratChaudhuri. Would it be possible to check to see if the article was in the training set? Solving the attribution problem is quite critical. As you can see, a lot of deep expert effort is expended on manually solving it for AI agents which significantly reduces the value proposition. Ofc, if the attribution problem is mostly solved, the utility would be very potent - even if the solutions are not novel.
Notable quote from the abstract: "I first heard about $H_8$ from Paul Erdős ..."
"...who learned about it from Heiko Harborth."

I wrote to Heiko Harborth and asked if the construction was original with him or if he heard about from yet somebody else. He writes: - He found the configuration himself and told Erdős at a conference about it - A subset of the configuration already appears as the third of the nine point configurations with 6 points and 3 distinct distances in H. Harborth and L. Piepmeyer: Three distinct distances in the plane. Geometria Dedicata 61(1996), 315-327, Figure 2

On February 14, a DeepMind prover agent found a Lean proof falsifying the second part of this conjecture. Of course, the conjecture had already been resolved, so the finding is not fundamentally novel. However, an interesting characteristic of our proof is that it constructs a counterexample with 8 points, smaller than the one @eigensolver gives in the thread below. The proof was discovered fully autonomously; the only problem-specific human input was the formal problem statement [link 1], drawn from Formal Conjectures [link 2]. The full Lean proof (compilable with Lean 4.22) is available here [link 3]. link 1: https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/pull/2397 link 2: https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures link 3: https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/blob/0aca4d71095301c0fd2dca32611b7addb2ea735c/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/1082.lean (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Your three postings look very interesting! Are you willing to describe your "DeepMind prover agent" in some more detail? Are Gemini 3 Pro instances involved there? Congrats, Ingo.
Thank you for the interest! We cannot yet share any details regarding the system, but hope to soon.
Congrats.

Here is a picture of the configuration of eight points. In words, one description of the configuration might be: Let four of the points be vertices of a square. Then, on each side of the square, construct an equilateral triangle. The remaining four points are the other vertices of those triangles. (The triangles can either all face outward or all face inward, but the resulting configuration is the same in both cases.) Also, note that the main conjecture is still open. Both this construction, and the one by eigensolver, disprove the second/"stronger" part of the conjecture.
Thanks for the pic! Yep, this is only partial solution.
Good catch! I have updated the initial comment to reflect that this is indeed only for the second part of the conjecture.
the pr also had a picture: https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/pull/2397 and a link to some sagemath code for it

Following @StijnC's idea, I seem to have found a counterexample to the second question. It consists of $42$ points: Image Link GeoGebra Link This counterexample is composed of two concentric regular $N$-gons ($N = 21$), denoted as $A_1A_2\dots A_N$ and $B_1B_2\dots B_N$ counterclockwisely, with inner and outer radius ratio equal to $r_0=\frac12\left(1-\sqrt{5-8\cos\frac{2\pi}{7}}\right)=0.4450418679\dots$, a root of $x^3-x^2-2x+1=0$. This set of points satisfies $|A_1A_4|=|A_1B_4|$ and $|B_1B_{10}|=|B_1 A_5|$, which degenerates distances, and for any $B_k$, the line $A_1 B_k$ does not pass through any other points. Due to symmetry, each point determines only $N−1 = 20$ distinct distances, thus constituting a counterexample to the second problem. The method I used involved solving quadratic equations in terms of $r$, of the form $|A_1 B_i| = |A_1 A_j|$ and $|B_1 B_k| = |B_1 A_l|$, obtaining solution sets $R_1$ and $R_2$, and then selecting $r$ from their intersection such that no three points are collinear. However, due to precision errors, I might have misjudged the solutions. I would be immensely grateful if someone could help verify this. By the way, all $(N, r)$ pairs I found are of the form: $$(42k+21, r_0),\quad (42k+21, 1-r_0)\quad (k\in\mathbb{N})$$ (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Great :) I checked that $(21,r_0)$ and $(21,1-r_0)$ are solutions with Maple. (the computations are purely goniometric) Since $42k+21$ is an odd multiple of $21$, the same result applies there, and so one has infinitely many examples, but all of them come from the core configuration with two $21$-gons. For $1-r_0$, one has $|A_1A_4|=|A_1B_4|$ and $|B_1B_7|=|B_1A_5|$ (the labeling in the geogebra file does not correspond with the usual numbering).
This counterexample has been verified in Lean 4 using Aristotle.

I tried to disprove the additional (stronger version of the) question, but didn't get there: take n odd, and two concentric regular n-gons $A_1A_2 \ldots A_n$ and $B_1B_2 \ldots B_n$ (common circumcenter $O$), with $O,A_i,B_i$ colinear for every $i$. By choosing $n$ and $|OA_i|/|OB_i|$ right, it might be that $|A_iA_j|=|A_i B_k|$ and $|B_iB_j|=|A_i B_\ell|$ for some $i,j,k,\ell$, while no $3$ points of the construction are colinear. If so, we would conclude. (satisfying 1 out of 2 of the first is easy) Update: checked for odd $n \le 250$, and no positive result.
A note that the second question is a stronger form of [982] (I'll add this remark). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
(Question prompted by construction with $n=21$ below.) When you checked odd $n\le 250$, were you assuming that $A_i$ and $B_i$ had exactly the same angle?

The incompleteness was that the check happened under the $|A_iA_j|=|A_iB_i|$ condition (i.e., not all possible combinations were taken) So I am very happy eigensolver did the effort to check all combinations, while I indeed only did a quick easy search.
